one_line_takeaway: A 4B-parameter open LLM, driven by an evolutionary propose-train-evaluate-mutate loop over executable loss functions, discovers task-specific machine-unlearning losses that match or beat established human-designed losses (NPO, SimNPO, GradDiff, RMU, etc.) on TOFU, MUSE, and WMDP -- including markedly better resistance to a WMDP-Bio relearning attack -- though the discovered losses transfer only partially across benchmark families and remain eventually reversible under sufficient adversarial fine-tuning.
key_finding: On TOFU-5% (Table 1, LLaMA2-7B-Chat) EvoMU attains the best model-utility (MU) score of 0.65 versus SimNPO 0.58, NPO 0.57, RKLD 0.56, IDKDPO 0.57, GradDiff 0.56, while reaching near-saturated forgetting (1-Rouge-L=1.00, 1-Prob=0.99, 1-Extraction-Strength=0.97). On TOFU-10% (Table 2, Llama3.2-1B-Instruct), EvoMU again attains the highest MU (0.58) versus SimNPO 0.56, NPO 0.57, AltPO 0.57, with baselines splitting into over-unlearning (e.g., IDKDPO MU=0.52, UNDIAL MU=0.51) and under-forgetting (RMU MU=0.57 but 1-Rouge-L only 0.50) failure modes. On WMDP (Table 4, Llama-3-8B-Instruct), EvoMU matches the best forgetting level (1-AccBio=0.75, tied with SimNPO) while improving utility to MMLU=0.53 versus SimNPO's MMLU=0.44 and NPO's 0.73/0.51. On MUSE (Table 3), EvoMU on MUSE News (LLaMA2-7B) cuts PrivLeak to 4.38 (versus NPO 108.91, SimNPO 72.93, GradDiff 108.12, Original -98.71, Retain-oracle 0.00) while matching the best retain-set KnowMem (47.98 vs. SimNPO 39.65); on MUSE Books (ICLM-7B) EvoMU achieves VerbMem=0.00 and KnowMem(Df)=7.33 while preserving retain KnowMem=53.58, versus SimNPO's VerbMem=0.00/KnowMem=0.00/retain-KnowMem=48.27 and NPO's retain-KnowMem=23.71. The random-sampling-without-evolution ablation (Fig. 3a) shows that even non-evolved LLM-sampled candidate losses can already match prior SotA baselines on TOFU-5%, and that evolutionary mutation is needed to push meaningfully past that. The hyperparameter grid-search ablation shows that even an extensively tuned SimNPO remains substantially below EvoMU on TOFU-10%, i.e., the gain is attributed by the authors to discovering new loss structure, not merely better-tuned hyperparameters of an existing loss family. The WMDP-Bio relearning experiment (Fig. 3b) shows EvoMU's forgetting metric (1-AccBio) stays nearly flat for roughly the first 800 of 1000 fine-tuning steps on 20% of the forget set, whereas SimNPO and NPO lose their forgetting effect rapidly from the start; all three eventually converge to similar (low) forgetting after enough steps.
core_contribution: EvoMU is an evolutionary automated-scientific-discovery (ASD) pipeline that treats LLM unlearning loss design as a search problem. A code-generating LLM (Qwen3-4B-Thinking) proposes candidate unlearning loss functions as executable PyTorch code (each with a self-specified training-epoch/step budget); each candidate is used to LoRA-fine-tune (rank r=8, alpha=16) a target base model on a forget set Df and retain set Dr, the resulting checkpoint is scored with a selection metric s(L) = 1/2*Utility + 1/2*Forget (via the Open-Unlearning evaluation protocol), and the top-K candidates are fed back to the LLM (loss code, per-epoch loss curves, epoch budget, full eval metrics) to be mutated into the next generation. Per run: 10 initial candidates -> top-5 -> 5 refinements each (25 candidates) -> top-3 -> 10 refinements each, for 65 losses tested total, taking 10-20 hours on 2x A100 (40GB). The paper's central empirical claim is that this small (4B-parameter), open, non-frontier LLM proposer is sufficient to discover task-specific unlearning losses that match or beat human-designed losses (GA, GradDiff, RMU, RKLD, AltPO, TaskVector, IDKDPO/IDKNLL, UNDIAL, NPO, SimNPO) across TOFU-5%, TOFU-10%, MUSE (News/Books) and WMDP, without a human in the loop.
threat_model: The paper is not an attack paper; it targets defensive/compliance capability -- reliably removing specified training data (sensitive, harmful, copyrighted, or hazardous content) from an already-trained autoregressive LLM without full retraining, so that (a) the forget-set content cannot be extracted/regurgitated, (b) membership in the forget set cannot be inferred from the model (PrivLeak / membership-leakage metric on MUSE, measured against a holdout non-member reference), and (c) hazardous domain knowledge (WMDP-Bio, a proxy for weapons-of-mass-destruction-relevant biological knowledge) is suppressed. The adversarial dimension addressed empirically is relearning/reversibility: the paper explicitly tests (Fig. 3b, following the protocol of Fan et al. 2024a) whether an adversary/downstream fine-tuner can cheaply restore 'forgotten' hazardous knowledge by fine-tuning on 20% of the WMDP-Bio forget set for 1000 steps at lr=1e-5 starting from unlearned checkpoints of SimNPO, NPO, and EvoMU. The paper's own Impact Statement flags a genuine dual-use risk: unlearning techniques (including EvoMU's automated loss search) could in principle be repurposed to strip safety behaviors from a model rather than remove hazardous knowledge, and warns that benchmark-driven optimization can create false confidence if the metrics do not reflect real deployment threat models.
limitations: The paper's own stated/implied limitations: (1) cross-benchmark transfer of a discovered loss is only partial -- the TOFU-5%-discovered loss transfers reasonably to TOFU-10% and WMDP but the authors report it degrades notably on MUSE News and MUSE Books, attributed to MUSE's qualitatively different failure mode (long-form verbatim memorization and membership leakage rather than fact-level QA), meaning EvoMU's losses are not shown to be universal and a fresh evolutionary run is generally needed per benchmark family. (2) The random-sampling ablation shows some LLM-proposed candidate losses are 'not sensible' -- they can increase log-likelihood of forget targets, i.e., actively counterproductive -- and are only filtered out via the evolutionary selection process across generations, implying the raw proposal distribution from a 4B model is noisy and selection-dependent. (3) The relearning experiment shows EvoMU's forgetting resistance is only relative/delayed, not absolute -- all three methods (EvoMU, SimNPO, NPO) eventually converge to similar low forgetting after sufficient fine-tuning steps, so EvoMU raises the cost of relearning attacks but does not eliminate the underlying reversibility problem the paper itself frames as a known unlearning weakness (citing Xu et al. 2025b/c, Hu et al. 2024a). (4) Compute cost, while modest relative to frontier-model ASD systems, is still substantial for a single discovered loss: each evolutionary run explores 65 candidate losses over 10-20 hours on 2x A100 GPUs, meaning per-benchmark cost is non-trivial for practitioners who cannot amortize it. (5) The search space is restricted to functions of four scalar batch statistics (no hidden states, gradients, or sampling access), which the authors note keeps discovery verifiable/efficient but also bounds the space of loss forms that can be found. (6) The Impact Statement itself flags that the technique is dual-use (could be applied to strip safety behaviors rather than hazardous knowledge) and that benchmark-driven optimization risks giving misleading confidence if the automatic metrics do not track real deployment threat models -- a limitation the authors surface but do not empirically probe.

datasets_used: MUSE News (Shi et al., 2024a) on LLaMA2-7B
MUSE Books (Shi et al., 2024a, Harry Potter corpus) on ICLM-7B
WMDP / WMDP-Bio (Li et al., 2024) on Llama-3-8B-Instruct, utility measured via MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2020)
TOFU Real Authors and World Facts out-of-distribution general-knowledge subsets (collateral-damage evaluation)
quantitative_results: TOFU-5% (Table 1): EvoMU MU=0.65 (best) vs. SimNPO 0.58, NPO 0.57, RKLD 0.56, IDKDPO 0.57, GradDiff 0.56; EvoMU forgetting 1-Rouge-L=1.00, 1-Prob=0.99, 1-Extraction-Strength=0.97
TOFU-10% (Table 2): EvoMU MU=0.58 (best) vs. SimNPO 0.56, NPO 0.57, AltPO 0.57, IDKDPO 0.52, UNDIAL 0.51; EvoMU forgetting 1-Rouge-L=0.96, 1-Prob=1.00, 1-Extraction-Strength=0.97
WMDP (Table 4, Llama-3-8B-Instruct): EvoMU 1-AccBio=0.75 / MMLU=0.53 vs. SimNPO 0.75/0.44, NPO 0.73/0.51, UnDIAL 0.65/0.45, GradDiff 0.73/0.26
MUSE News (Table 3, LLaMA2-7B): EvoMU PrivLeak=4.38, KnowMem(Dr)=47.98 vs. NPO PrivLeak=108.91/KnowMem 37.58, SimNPO PrivLeak=72.93/KnowMem 39.65, Retain-oracle PrivLeak=0.00/KnowMem 53.79
MUSE Books (Table 3, ICLM-7B): EvoMU VerbMem=0.00, KnowMem(Df)=7.33, KnowMem(Dr)=53.58 vs. SimNPO VerbMem=0.00/KnowMem(Df)=0.00/KnowMem(Dr)=48.27, NPO KnowMem(Dr)=23.71

Cross-benchmark transfer: 'EvoMU (TOFU-5%)' loss applied unchanged reaches WMDP 1-AccBio=0.67/MMLU=0.62 and TOFU-10% MU=0.58, but MUSE News PrivLeak=-99.78 (near-original, i.e. degraded) and MUSE Books KnowMem(Df)=28.22 (worse retention of forgetting than in-domain-discovered EvoMU)
Evolutionary search scale: N=10 initial candidates -> top-5 -> 5 refinements each (25) -> top-3 -> 10 refinements each, 65 total losses evaluated per run, 10-20 hours on 2x NVIDIA A100 (40GB)

Relearning attack (Fig. 3b, WMDP-Bio): fine-tuning on 20% of forget set, 1000 steps, lr=1e-5 -- EvoMU's 1-AccBio stays nearly flat for ~800 steps while SimNPO and NPO degrade rapidly from step 0; all converge to similar low forgetting by step 1000
baselines_compared: IDKDPO / IDKNLL ('I don't know' targets, DPO-style or NLL)
Original (no unlearning) and Retain (oracle model retrained without forget set) references
